questions.question: Explain the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Preemptive allows forced removal of CPU from running process
Non-preemptive runs process until completion or voluntary yield
Examples: Round Robin (preemptive), FCFS (non-preemptive)
Preemptive provides better response time but adds overhead

questions.question: Define and explain any three scheduling criteria used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms.
keyPoints: CPU Utilization: Percentage of time CPU is busy
Throughput: Number of processes completed per unit time
Turnaround Time: Total time from submission to completion
Waiting Time: Total time spent in ready queue
Response Time: Time from arrival to first CPU allocation

questions.question: What is a context switch? Explain its components and the overhead involved.
keyPoints: Context switch saves state of one process and loads another
Components: Save PCB, update queues, load new PCB, switch address space
Overhead: Dispatch latency - time spent in switching not doing useful work
Higher context switch rate means more CPU time wasted on overhead

questions.question: Describe the three types of schedulers in an operating system with their functions.
keyPoints: Long-term scheduler: Selects jobs from job pool to ready queue, controls degree of multiprogramming
Short-term scheduler: Selects process from ready queue to CPU, must be fast
Medium-term scheduler: Swaps processes in/out of memory to balance load
Each operates at different frequencies with different goals

questions.question: What is the convoy effect in FCFS scheduling? How can it be overcome?
keyPoints: Convoy effect: Short processes wait behind long process in ready queue
Causes poor CPU utilization and increased average waiting time
Solution: Use preemptive scheduling or priority-based scheduling
Round Robin eliminates convoy effect by giving each process time quantum

questions.question: Explain the difference between CPU-bound and I/O-bound processes. How does the scheduler handle both?
keyPoints: CPU-bound processes have long CPU bursts, less I/O
I/O-bound processes have short CPU bursts, frequent I/O
Scheduler should balance them for better throughput
I/O-bound processes should get priority to keep devices busy while CPU-bound wait

questions.question: What is starvation in process scheduling? Under which scheduling algorithm does it occur and how can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Starvation: Process waits indefinitely for CPU allocation
Occurs in priority scheduling and SJF when low priority processes keep waiting
Prevention: Aging - gradually increase priority of waiting processes
Preemptive priority can also cause starvation for low-priority processes

questions.question:
Consider the following set of processes with their arrival times and burst times:
Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P1 | 0 | 8
P2 | 1 | 4
P3 | 2 | 9
P4 | 3 | 5

Calculate average waiting time and turnaround time using FCFS and SJF (non-preemptive) scheduling algorithms. Show all steps.
keyPoints: FCFS: P1→P2→P3→P4 order
Calculate completion time for each: P1=8, P2=12, P3=21, P4=26
Turnaround time = Completion - Arrival: P1=8, P2=11, P3=19, P4=23
Waiting time = Turnaround - Burst: P1=0, P2=7, P3=10, P4=18
Average waiting time FCFS = (0+7+10+18)/4 = 8.75
SJF: P1(8), P2(4), P4(5), P3(9) order after considering arrivals
Calculate completion: P1=8, P2=12, P4=17, P3=26
Average waiting time SJF = (0+7+5+8)/4 = 5.0
Compare results and conclude SJF gives better average waiting time

questions.question:
Explain the Round Robin scheduling algorithm. If time quantum = 4 units, calculate the waiting time for each process given:
Process | Burst Time
P1 | 10
P2 | 5
P3 | 8

Assume all processes arrive at time 0.
keyPoints: Round Robin: Each process gets time quantum in cyclic order
Execution sequence: P1(4) → P2(4) → P3(4) → P1(4) → P3(4) → P1(2)
Completion times: P1=18, P2=9, P3=17
Turnaround: P1=18, P2=9, P3=17
Waiting: P1=8, P2=4, P3=9
Average waiting time = (8+4+9)/3 = 7 units
Discuss impact of time quantum on performance

questions.question: What is a dispatcher? Explain the functions of dispatcher and compare it with scheduler. Also define dispatch latency.
keyPoints: Dispatcher gives CPU control to selected process by short-term scheduler
Functions: Context switch, switch to user mode, jump to program location
Dispatcher operates on every context switch unlike scheduler which decides selection
Dispatch latency = time to stop one process and start another running
Dispatcher is part of OS kernel, highly optimized for speed
Key difference: Scheduler decides WHICH process; Dispatcher gives CPU to that process

questions.question: Explain priority scheduling algorithm. Discuss the problem of starvation and how aging can solve it with an example.
keyPoints: Priority scheduling: Each process assigned priority, highest priority runs first
Can be preemptive or non-preemptive
Starvation: Low priority processes may wait indefinitely if high priority keep arriving
Aging: Gradually increase priority of processes that wait long
Example: Process with priority 10 waiting for 5 seconds gets priority increased to 11
After more waiting, priority increases to 12, eventually becomes highest
This ensures no process waits forever and system remains responsive to all
